did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a modified deep learning model called SBXception (a shallower and broader variant of the Xception network) to classify skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset. The task was skin cancer classification, achieving 96.97% accuracy on a holdout test set.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel deep learning model called SBXception, describes its architecture modifications (shallower and broader Xception), details the experimental methodology using the HAM10000 dataset, and reports original results including accuracy (96.97%), parameter reduction (54.27%), and class-specific performance metrics. It is not a review or survey.

title: SBXception: A Shallower and Broader Xception Architecture for Efficient Classification of Skin Lesions
brief_summary: This study proposes SBXception, a shallower and broader variant of the Xception deep learning architecture, to improve the efficiency and accuracy of skin cancer classification. The model was trained and evaluated using the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images of skin lesions across seven categories. The primary evaluation approach involved fine-tuning the modified architecture on a holdout test set and comparing its performance against the base Xception model and other state-of-the-art methods. The proposed SBXception model achieved a weighted average accuracy of 96.97% on the test set. Additionally, the architecture demonstrated significant efficiency gains, reducing the number of training parameters by 54.27% and decreasing training time by 30.46% compared to the original Xception network. These results indicate that modifying the depth and breadth of the Xception architecture can effectively enhance skin lesion classification performance while maintaining computational efficiency.

all_task_types.target: Seven categories of skin lesions (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant types and specific lesion classes) rather than continuous values. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score, and MCC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of 10,015 dermatoscopic images of skin lesions acquired from individuals across various demographic regions, age groups, ethnicities, and geographical locations. Each image is linked to a unique patient record via a 7-digit lesion_id.

use.training: Used for training the SBXception model. The original training set was augmented to 37,197 images for the final model training.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance on a holdout test set of 2,004 images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, parameters, training time) without statistical inference.

HAM10000.experiment: SBXception (Xception-sb3) vs Base Xception vs Xception-m1
HAM10000.notes: DF (Dermatofibroma) was identified as the most challenging class with the lowest accuracy (0.9532), recall (0.7175), F1 (0.8153), and MCC (0.7989). VASC (Vascular skin lesions) achieved the best performance across all metrics. The SBXception model (Xception-sb3) was selected as optimal based on the balance of accuracy and parameter count compared to other Xception-sbN variations.

architecture_used.main_model: SBXception (specifically Xception-sb3, a shallower and broader variant of the Xception network)

architecture_details.modifications: Reduced depth and increased breadth. Specifically, the middle flow depth was reduced to one repetition of nine-layer RCB (ReLU, Conv2D, Batch Normalization) structures (Xception-m1) for depth optimization, and then broadened by adding an 'add' layer to fuse horizontal channels from expanded branches. The final selected model, Xception-sb3, contains three RCB layers in the broadened structure.
architecture_details.layer_composition: Contains three RCB (ReLU, Conv2D, Batch Normalization) layers in the broadened core structure.
architecture_details.optimization_strategy: Network width increased by expanding a branch of the core structure and connecting outputs with an add layer, avoiding 1x1 convolution in residual connections.

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Reduced by a factor of 1/10 if loss reduction was not evident for more than ten epochs
training_details.data_augmentation: Applied to training set (augmented from 8,011 to 37,197 images)
training_details.dataset_split: 80% training, 20% testing; 20% of training used for validation

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing: Original images (600 × 450) were resized to 229 × 229 for efficient processing.
Data augmentation: Applied to the training set, increasing the number of training images from 8,011 to 37,197.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training set to address data imbalance. The original training set of 8,011 images was augmented to 37,197 images. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation, flipping, zoom ranges) were not explicitly reported in the provided text, only the final augmented image counts per class (e.g., AKIEC: 262 to 5478, BCC: 411 to 5168, BKL: 879 to 5515, DF: 91 to 4324, MEL: 891 to 5088, NV: 5364 to 6306, VASC: 113 to 5317).

conclusions: Reducing the depth and expanding the breadth of the Xception network architecture significantly improves its performance in skin cancer categorization.
The proposed SBXception model (specifically Xception-sb3) achieved a weighted average accuracy of 96.97% on the HAM10000 holdout test set, outperforming the base Xception model.

The SBXception architecture reduces the number of training parameters by 54.27% and decreases training time by 30.46% compared to the base Xception model, demonstrating enhanced computational efficiency.
Classification of the HAM10000 dataset does not benefit from deeper networks; instead, shallower and broader architectures are more suitable for this task.
The Xception-sb3 architecture, containing three RCB layers in the broadened structure, yielded the highest scores considering both accuracy and parameter count among the tested variations.
Dermatofibroma (DF) was identified as the most challenging class to classify, while Vascular skin lesions (VASC) achieved the best performance across all metrics.
Modifying the depth and breadth of the Xception architecture can effectively enhance skin lesion classification performance while maintaining computational efficiency.
important_remarks: The study is limited to the HAM10000 dataset; the authors explicitly state that performance needs to be evaluated on other datasets to ensure generalizability.
The model was only tested on seven specific skin lesion classes, excluding other potential dermatological conditions.
The study did not address model interpretability, which limits its immediate clinical applicability.
The study did not analyze demographic bias or skin color diversity, acknowledging this as a limitation for ensuring detection across different populations.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on a holdout test set from the training dataset source.

Dermatofibroma (DF) was identified as the most challenging class with the lowest accuracy (0.9532) and recall (0.7175), indicating potential bias or difficulty in classifying this specific lesion type.
The paper did not report any formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance metrics.
Data augmentation was applied to the training set (increasing images from 8,011 to 37,197), but specific geometric or color transform parameters were not explicitly reported.
